title: Cancer of the Skin
authors: Brendan D. Curti, John T. Vetto, Sancy A. Leachman

items.title: How the ABCDEs and 'ugly duckling' guide early melanoma detection
items.content:
Early detection of melanoma before invasion and metastasis is essential, and most melanomas arise in the skin where they are detectable.
The ABCDEs flag suspicious pigmented lesions: Asymmetry (benign lesions are usually symmetric), Border irregularity (most nevi have clear-cut borders), Color variegation (benign lesions usually have uniform pigment), Diameter >6 mm (pencil-eraser size), and Evolving (any change in size, shape, color, elevation, or new bleeding/itching/crusting).
In addition, any nevus that appears atypical and different from the rest of a person's nevi (an 'ugly duckling') should be considered suspicious.
The entire skin surface, scalp, mucous membranes, and nails should be examined, aided by a dermatoscope.
keyPoints: ABCDE: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variegation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolving
The 'ugly duckling' (a nevus unlike the patient's others) is suspicious
Examine all skin, scalp, mucosa, and nails; dermoscopy improves pattern assessment
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS: "applying the ABCDEs: asymmetry...border irregularity...color variegation...diameter >6 mm...and evolving..."

items.title: Why the MC1R red-hair phenotype increases melanoma risk
items.content:
Pigment biology shapes melanoma risk through the melanocortin-1 receptor.
UVR stimulates α-MSH, the ligand for MC1R, a G-protein-coupled receptor that regulates the amount and type of melanin produced; MC1R is highly polymorphic, and many of its ~80 variants cause loss of signaling and production of non-photoprotective red/yellow pheomelanin rather than photoprotective brown/black eumelanin.
The resulting red-hair-color phenotype (light skin, red hair, freckles, sun sensitivity) carries increased melanoma risk.
Beyond weak UV shielding, increased pheomelanin also contributes a UV-independent carcinogenic effect via oxidative damage and reduced DNA-damage repair.
keyPoints: MC1R variants → pheomelanin (red/yellow, poorly photoprotective) instead of eumelanin
Red-hair-color phenotype: light skin, red hair, freckles, sun sensitivity, ↑melanoma risk
Pheomelanin also adds UV-independent carcinogenicity (oxidative damage, impaired repair)
items.reference: GENETIC SUSCEPTIBILITY TO MELANOMA: "The red hair color (RHC) phenotype produced by MC1R mutations includes lightly colored skin, red hair, freckles, increased sun sensitivity, and increased risk of melanoma."

items.title: How CDKN2A mutations drive hereditary melanoma
items.content:
The major familial melanoma gene disables two tumor-suppressor pathways at once.
Approximately 20–40% of hereditary melanomas are due to germline CDKN2A mutations, and 70% of all cutaneous melanomas have mutations/deletions of the CDKN2A locus (chromosome 9p21).
CDKN2A encodes two proteins from alternate reading frames — p16, which inhibits CDK4/6-mediated inactivation of the retinoblastoma (RB) protein, and ARF (p14ARF), which inhibits MDM2-mediated degradation of p53 — so its loss inactivates both the RB and p53 pathways that control cell-cycle entry.
Melanoma-prone families with CDKN2A mutations also have an increased risk of pancreatic cancer.
keyPoints: CDKN2A (9p21) is the major familial melanoma gene; affected in ~70% of cutaneous melanomas
Encodes p16 (RB pathway) and ARF/p14 (p53 pathway) — loss disables both tumor suppressors
CDKN2A families also have increased pancreatic cancer risk
items.reference: GENETIC SUSCEPTIBILITY TO MELANOMA: "The loss of CDKN2A results in inactivation of two critical tumor-suppressor pathways, RB and p53, which control entry of cells into the cell cycle."

items.title: How a suspicious pigmented lesion should be biopsied
items.content:
Biopsy technique must allow accurate thickness measurement.
An excisional biopsy with 1–3 mm margins (narrow-margin excision) is suggested, as it permits histologic assessment, accurate thickness measurement if melanoma, and is definitive treatment if benign.
For large lesions or anatomically difficult sites (face, hands, feet), or when malignancy suspicion is low, an incisional biopsy (shave, saucerization, or punch) sampling the most nodular/darkest area is acceptable — and incisional biopsy does not appear to facilitate melanoma spread.
All biopsies should be deep enough to include the deepest component; the report should include Breslow thickness, mitotic rate, ulceration, lymphovascular/neural invasion, regression, microsatellitosis, and margins.
keyPoints: Preferred: excisional biopsy with 1–3 mm margins (accurate thickness, definitive if benign)
Incisional biopsy acceptable for large/difficult lesions and does NOT spread melanoma
Report Breslow thickness, mitotic rate, ulceration, lymphovascular invasion, and margins
items.reference: Biopsy: "An excisional biopsy with 1- to 3-mm margins (narrow-margin excision) is suggested."

items.title: Why Breslow thickness and ulceration are the key prognostic factors
items.content:
The primary tumor's depth and surface integrity dominate prognosis.
The best predictor of recurrence is Breslow thickness, followed by ulceration; together they make up the T stage for melanoma.
Breslow thickness is the greatest thickness measured from the top of the epidermal granular layer (or the ulcer base) to the bottom of the tumor.
Other adverse factors include high mitotic rate, lymphovascular invasion, microsatellite/in-transit metastases, nodal involvement, elevated serum LDH, and metastases to brain, liver, or bone; favorable primary sites are the forearm and leg, and unfavorable sites are the scalp, hands, feet, and mucous membranes.
keyPoints: Breslow thickness is the best recurrence predictor, then ulceration (together = T stage)
Other adverse factors: high mitotic rate, lymphovascular invasion, nodal disease, high LDH
Favorable sites: forearm/leg; unfavorable: scalp, hands, feet, mucosa
items.reference: PROGNOSTIC FACTORS: "The best predictor of recurrence is Breslow thickness, followed by ulceration, which together make up the T stage for melanoma."

items.title: How BRAF, RAS, and NF1 mutations classify cutaneous melanoma
items.content:
Driver mutations in the MAP kinase pathway define genomic subtypes and therapeutic targets.
Cutaneous melanomas have one of the highest somatic mutation rates (>10/Mb), most showing a UV signature; a genomic classification is based on the most prevalent mutated genes — BRAF, RAS, and NF1 — plus a triple wild-type group.
NRAS is mutated in ~20% of melanomas, and activating BRAF mutations occur in 40–50% of cutaneous melanomas (and most benign nevi), most commonly the V600E (valine-to-glutamate) point mutation; V600E is more common in younger patients and intermittently sun-exposed skin, and less common in chronically sun-damaged skin.
Neither mutation alone is sufficient (often accompanied by TERT mutations); melanomas can also alter the PI3K/AKT pathway (AKT amplification, PTEN loss) and NF1 (10–15%).
keyPoints: Genomic classes: BRAF, RAS, NF1, and triple wild-type; pathways = MAP kinase and PI3K/AKT
BRAF mutated in 40–50% (most commonly V600E); NRAS ~20%; NF1 10–15%
V600E more common in younger patients/intermittent sun; rarer in chronically sun-damaged skin
items.reference: CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOGENESIS (Genomic): "somatic activating BRAF mutations are found in most benign nevi and 40–50% of cutaneous melanomas...The BRAF mutation is most commonly a T→A point mutation that results in a valine-to-glutamate amino acid substitution (V600E)."

items.title: How wide-excision margins are determined by tumor thickness
items.content:
Surgical margins for melanoma scale with Breslow thickness.
NCCN recommends radial margins of 0.5–1.0 cm for melanoma in situ, 1 cm for invasive melanoma up to 1 mm thick, 1–2 cm for >1.01–2 mm, and 2 cm for >2 mm.
Smaller margins may be used for 'anatomically constrained' locations (face, hands, feet, genitalia) to limit morbidity, and Mohs micrographic surgery with immunostaining can be advantageous there and for extensive in-situ disease.
Including subcutaneous fat in the specimen facilitates thickness measurement; excision should go to fascia, with fascial resection for thick (T4) lesions.
keyPoints: Margins: in situ 0.5–1 cm; ≤1 mm 1 cm; 1.01–2 mm 1–2 cm; >2 mm 2 cm
Smaller margins / Mohs for anatomically constrained sites (face, hands, feet, genitalia)
Include subcutaneous fat; excise to fascia (fascial resection for T4)
items.reference: MANAGEMENT OF CLINICALLY LOCALIZED MELANOMA: "a primary MIS, 0.5–1.0 cm; invasive up to 1 mm thick, 1 cm; >1.01–2 mm, 1–2 cm; and >2 mm, 2 cm."

items.title: How sentinel lymph node biopsy is used in melanoma staging
items.content:
SLNB provides prognostic information to identify candidates for adjuvant therapy.
The first draining (sentinel) node is located by injecting blue dye and a gamma-emitting radioisotope around the primary, then identified with a handheld gamma detector and removed for histopathology with immunostains (S100, HMB45, MART-1, MelanA).
NCCN recommends SLNB for patients with a ≥10% chance of nodal tumor — tumors >1 mm thick (T2) or T1 tumors with ulceration (T1b) — and considers it for 5–10% risk (0.75–1.0 mm, transected/regressed tumors, lymphovascular invasion).
SLNB is not recommended when the risk is ≤5% (melanoma ≤0.75 mm without high-risk features); there, wide excision alone is usual definitive therapy.
keyPoints: SLNB recommended for ≥10% nodal risk: tumors >1 mm (T2) or ulcerated T1 (T1b)
Consider for 5–10% risk; not recommended for ≤5% risk (≤0.75 mm, no high-risk features)
Provides prognosis and identifies candidates for adjuvant therapy
items.reference: MANAGEMENT OF CLINICALLY LOCALIZED MELANOMA: "NCCN guidelines recommend SLNB for patients with a 10% or greater chance of having tumor in the node. This includes patients with tumors >1 mm thick (T2) or T1 tumors that have ulceration (T1b)."

items.title: Why completion lymphadenectomy is no longer routine after a positive sentinel node
items.content:
A positive sentinel node no longer mandates removing all regional nodes.
Complete lymphadenectomy following identification of a positive sentinel lymph node improves relapse-free but not overall survival, so it is no longer offered routinely — avoiding the morbidity of regional node dissection in most patients.
It should still be considered in patients who cannot comply with follow-up or who forgo adjuvant therapy.
Patients not undergoing completion dissection should have nodal-bed surveillance with examination and imaging (ultrasound or CT) every 4–6 months for about 3 years to detect isolated nodal-bed progression.
keyPoints: Completion lymphadenectomy improves relapse-free but NOT overall survival → not routine
Consider it only if the patient cannot follow up or declines adjuvant therapy
Otherwise do nodal-bed surveillance (exam + ultrasound/CT) every 4–6 months for ~3 years
items.reference: MANAGEMENT OF CLINICALLY LOCALIZED MELANOMA: "Complete lymphadenectomy following identification of a positive sentinel lymph node improves relapse-free but not overall survival, and therefore, it is no longer offered routinely."

items.title: How adjuvant therapy is selected for high-risk and stage III melanoma
items.content:
Effective adjuvant systemic therapy now reduces recurrence across high-risk and resected stage III/IV disease.
Patients with thick and/or ulcerated stage IIB or IIC melanoma have a significant recurrence risk (13–18% probability of death at 5 years), and adjuvant anti-PD-1 immunotherapy (pembrolizumab or nivolumab) for 1 year significantly reduces recurrence or death and is now standard of care.
For resected stage III disease, adjuvant therapy should be offered — options are anti-PD-1 (nivolumab or pembrolizumab) or, for BRAF V600-mutant tumors, BRAF/MEK inhibitor targeted therapy — both conferring disease-free and overall survival benefit.
For bulky stage IIIC/IIID disease, neoadjuvant immunotherapy before resection improves event-free and overall survival over upfront surgery.
keyPoints: Stage IIB/IIC: 1 year of adjuvant anti-PD-1 (pembrolizumab/nivolumab) is standard
Resected stage III: adjuvant anti-PD-1, or BRAF/MEK inhibitors for BRAF V600-mutant tumors
Bulky stage IIIC/IIID: neoadjuvant immunotherapy before resection improves outcomes
items.reference: MANAGEMENT OF CLINICALLY LOCALIZED MELANOMA: "Adjuvant anti-PD-1 immunotherapy using pembrolizumab or nivolumab for 1 year significantly reduces the risk of melanoma recurrence or death in stage IIB or IIC melanoma and has become a standard of care."

items.title: How immune checkpoint blockade transformed metastatic melanoma
items.content:
Blocking inhibitory T-cell receptors reactivates antitumor immunity.
Inhibitory checkpoints (CTLA-4, PD-1, LAG-3) are upregulated on T cells and, during chronic antigen exposure in cancer, cause T-cell exhaustion; ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) was the first agent shown to improve survival in metastatic melanoma but has been supplanted by anti-PD-1.
The PD-1 blockers nivolumab and pembrolizumab are approved for advanced melanoma, and combination ipilimumab + nivolumab gives superior antitumor activity (objective response ~56%) versus ipilimumab alone; anti-LAG-3 (relatlimab) plus nivolumab gives comparable responses with fewer side effects.
Before 2010, 15-year survival of stage IV melanoma was <10%; with these therapies it is now nearly 50% for M1a/M1b disease, and progression-free survival beyond 2 years predicts long survival and possible cure.
keyPoints: Checkpoints CTLA-4/PD-1/LAG-3 cause T-cell exhaustion; blocking them reactivates immunity
Anti-PD-1 (nivolumab/pembrolizumab) supplanted anti-CTLA-4; ipi+nivo ~56% response
Relatlimab (anti-LAG-3) + nivolumab: comparable response, fewer side effects
items.reference: IMMUNOTHERAPY: "Combination T-cell checkpoint therapy, blocking both inhibitory pathways with ipilimumab and nivolumab, leads to superior antitumor activity compared to treatment with either agent alone."

items.title: Why tumor mutational burden predicts checkpoint response better than PD-L1
items.content:
Selecting patients for immunotherapy is imperfect, but mutational burden helps.
Patients with >5% PD-1 expression on tumor T cells derive similar benefit from nivolumab monotherapy, but using PD-1/PD-L1 expression to select therapy is problematic because some melanomas with no detectable expression still respond.
Tumor mutational burden (TMB) correlates with a higher probability of objective response and longer progression-free survival on checkpoint antibodies in melanoma and other solid tumors.
In melanoma, TMB appears to be a more robust predictor of response than PD-L1 expression — consistent with high-mutation tumors generating more neoantigens recognized as foreign.
keyPoints: PD-L1/PD-1 expression imperfectly predicts response (expression-negative tumors can respond)
Higher TMB → higher response rate and longer progression-free survival on checkpoint therapy
TMB is a more robust predictor than PD-L1 in melanoma
items.reference: IMMUNOTHERAPY: "TMB appears to be a more robust predictor of response compared to PD-L1 expression in melanoma."

items.title: How immune-related adverse events are managed in melanoma immunotherapy
items.content:
Checkpoint antibodies disrupt normal immune regulation, producing organ-specific autoimmunity.
The most common immune-related adverse events are skin rash and diarrhea (sometimes severe, life-threatening colitis), but toxicity can affect almost any organ — thyroiditis, hypophysitis, hepatitis, nephritis, pneumonitis, myocarditis, neuritis.
Severity and frequency are greatest with combined anti-CTLA-4 + anti-PD-1, then anti-CTLA-4, then anti-LAG-3 + anti-PD-1, and least with anti-PD-1 monotherapy.
Vigilance, interrupting therapy, and early steroids or other immunosuppressants (anti-TNF antibodies, mycophenolate) mitigate toxicity — and crucially, using immunosuppressants to control toxicity does NOT diminish antitumor activity, with benefit seen even in patients who must stop immunotherapy.
keyPoints: Common irAEs: rash and diarrhea/colitis; can involve any organ (thyroid, pituitary, liver, lung, heart)
Toxicity: highest with ipi+nivo, lowest with anti-PD-1 monotherapy
Treat with steroids/immunosuppressants — this does NOT reduce antitumor efficacy
items.reference: IMMUNOTHERAPY: "Using immunosuppressive agents to mitigate toxicity does not diminish antitumor activity..."

items.title: Why BRAF and MEK inhibitors are combined for BRAF-mutant melanoma
items.content:
Single-agent BRAF inhibition triggers rapid pathway reactivation and resistance.
The RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK pathway is mutated in ~50% of melanomas; BRAF inhibitors (vemurafenib, dabrafenib, encorafenib) induce regression of BRAF V600-mutant melanomas, but monotherapy has been supplanted by combined BRAF + MEK inhibition because melanomas rapidly adapt by reactivating the MAP kinase pathway.
Combined therapy (dabrafenib+trametinib, vemurafenib+cobimetinib, encorafenib+binimetinib) improves progression-free and overall survival over BRAF monotherapy.
Some patients achieve long disease control, but the major limitation is acquired resistance, usually via MAPK reactivation (RAS mutations or BRAF amplification); patients who progress are candidates for immunotherapy.
keyPoints: BRAF V600-mutant melanoma: combine BRAF + MEK inhibitors (better than BRAF alone)
Combination counters MAP kinase pathway reactivation that drives adaptation
Main limitation is acquired resistance (MAPK reactivation) → switch to immunotherapy
items.reference: TARGETED THERAPY: "Monotherapy with BRAF inhibitors has been supplanted with combined BRAF and MEK inhibition to address the rapid adaptation of melanomas that use MAP kinase pathway reactivation..."

items.title: Why BRAF inhibitor monotherapy causes squamous cell skin cancers
items.content:
A paradoxical pathway effect explains a class-specific toxicity.
A class-specific side effect of BRAF inhibitor monotherapy is hyperproliferative skin lesions, including well-differentiated squamous cell skin cancers (and keratoacanthomas) in up to 25% of patients.
This occurs because BRAF inhibition paradoxically activates the MAP kinase pathway in BRAF wild-type cells; adding a MEK inhibitor blocks this activation, which is why these lesions are infrequent during combined BRAF/MEK therapy.
These treatment-induced SCCs have not been reported to metastasize, and BRAF/MEK inhibitors can be continued safely after simple excision.
keyPoints: BRAF inhibitor monotherapy → squamous cell cancers/keratoacanthomas in up to 25%
Mechanism: paradoxical MAP kinase activation in BRAF wild-type cells (blocked by MEK inhibitor)
Lesions don't metastasize; excise and continue BRAF/MEK therapy
items.reference: TARGETED THERAPY: "A class-specific side effect of BRAF inhibitor monotherapy is the development of hyperproliferative skin lesions, including well-differentiated squamous cell skin cancers (SCCs) in up to 25% of patients."

items.title: How to approach a patient with newly diagnosed metastatic melanoma
items.content:
Molecular testing and immunotherapy-first sequencing guide treatment.
At diagnosis of stage IV disease, the tumor should be tested for BRAF and c-kit mutations (a metastatic lesion is preferred, but any sample suffices given little primary-metastasis discordance); treatment algorithms start with BRAF status.
For BRAF wild-type tumors, immunotherapy is recommended; for BRAF-mutant tumors, a randomized study showed that immunotherapy (anti-CTLA-4 + anti-PD-1) followed by BRAF/MEK targeted therapy at progression gave better overall survival than the reverse sequence — so survival data favor initial combined checkpoint immunotherapy.
Activating c-kit mutations (more common in mucosal/acral melanoma) respond to imatinib (objective response ~29%, usually transient), and metastatic uveal melanoma (HLA-A*02:01) can be treated with tebentafusp.
keyPoints: Test stage IV tumor for BRAF and c-kit; start the algorithm with BRAF status
Survival favors immunotherapy first, then BRAF/MEK at progression (for BRAF-mutant tumors)
c-kit-mutant (mucosal/acral) → imatinib; uveal melanoma (HLA-A*02:01) → tebentafusp
items.reference: INITIAL APPROACH TO PATIENT WITH METASTATIC DISEASE: "immunotherapy followed by targeted therapy conferred statistically significant better overall survival at 2 years..."

items.title: How the non-melanoma skin cancers differ in cause and behavior
items.content:
BCC, SCC, and Merkel cell carcinoma are the main NMSCs, all driven largely by UV but with distinct biology.
NMSCs are the most common cancers in the US (>5.3 million/year; BCC ~80%, SCC ~18%); BCC has very low metastatic potential (0.1%) but ~40% 5-year risk of recurrence/new primary, while SCC metastasizes in 0.3–5.2% (higher on lip/ear, scars, mucosa).
The molecular drivers differ: SCC involves UV damage to p53 and N-RAS, whereas BCC primarily involves hedgehog-pathway genes — usually loss of the tumor suppressor PTCH1, which normally inhibits SMO signaling.
Immunosuppression markedly increases risk: solid-organ transplant recipients have a 65-fold increase in SCC and 10-fold in BCC, and their SCCs are particularly aggressive.
keyPoints: BCC (~80%): very low metastasis (0.1%) but ~40% 5-yr recurrence/new primary; SCC (~18%): metastasizes 0.3–5.2%
BCC = hedgehog pathway (PTCH1 loss → SMO signaling); SCC = p53/N-RAS UV damage
Transplant immunosuppression: 65× SCC, 10× BCC, and SCCs behave aggressively
items.reference: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY: "chronically immunosuppressed solid organ transplant recipients have a 65-fold increase in SCC and a 10-fold increase in BCC."

items.title: How basal cell carcinoma presents and is treated
items.content:
BCC is a slowly enlarging, locally invasive tumor with characteristic subtypes.
Superficial BCC is an erythematous scaling plaque (mimics eczema/psoriasis); nodular BCC is a pearly nodule with telangiectasias, rolled borders, and a central crust (pigmented variant can mimic melanoma); morpheaform/infiltrative/micronodular subtypes are scar-like, indurated, and the most aggressive with indistinct borders.
Treatments include electrodesiccation and curettage (most frequent for superficial/low-risk nodular BCC), wide excision for aggressive subtypes, and Mohs micrographic surgery (cure rates >98%) for recurrent or cosmetically sensitive/high-risk locations (eyelids, lips, ears, nose, digits).
For metastatic or advanced BCC not amenable to surgery/radiation, hedgehog pathway inhibitors (vismodegib, sonidegib) induce regression in ~50% (not curative); cemiplimab can be used after progression.
keyPoints: Subtypes: superficial (plaque), nodular (pearly + telangiectasia), morpheaform (scar-like, aggressive)
ED&C for low-risk; Mohs (>98% cure) for recurrent/high-risk/cosmetic sites
Advanced/metastatic BCC: hedgehog inhibitors (vismodegib/sonidegib), then cemiplimab
items.reference: BASAL CELL CARCINOMA (Treatment): "Systemic therapy with a targeted hedgehog pathway inhibitor, such as vismodegib or sonidegib, is indicated for patients with metastatic or advanced BCC..."

items.title: How squamous cell carcinoma presents and which sites metastasize
items.content:
Cutaneous SCC ranges from indolent to aggressive, with site-dependent metastatic risk.
SCC typically appears as an ulcerated erythematous nodule or erosion on sun-exposed skin (often mistaken for a wart or callus); keratoacanthoma is a rapidly growing dome-shaped papule with a central keratotic crater that, although it may regress, should be treated as SCC (and occurs in 15–25% of patients on BRAF inhibitor monotherapy).
Actinic keratoses and cheilitis are premalignant; SCC in situ (Bowen's disease) is the intraepidermal form.
SCC arising on sun-damaged skin has lower metastatic potential, but lip and ear SCC metastasize in 13% and 11% respectively, and SCC in scars/chronic ulcers/mucosa, recurrent SCC (30% metastatic risk), and tumors in immunosuppressed patients behave aggressively; cemiplimab (anti-PD-1) is the systemic therapy of choice for advanced disease.
keyPoints: SCC = ulcerated nodule on sun-exposed skin; keratoacanthoma is a fast-growing crateriform variant (treat as SCC)
Higher metastatic risk: lip (13%), ear (11%), scars/ulcers/mucosa, recurrent SCC (30%), immunosuppressed
Advanced SCC: cemiplimab (anti-PD-1) is the systemic therapy of choice
items.reference: SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA (Treatment): "Cemiplimab, a monoclonal antibody targeting PD-1, has become the systemic therapy of choice, inducing tumor regression in 47% of patients with advanced disease."

items.title: How Merkel cell carcinoma is recognized (AEIOU) and treated
items.content:
Merkel cell carcinoma is an uncommon but aggressive neuroendocrine skin cancer with a viral cause.
MCC arises from Merkel cells (neuroendocrine pressure receptors), usually as a rapidly enlarging flesh-colored (or red/blue) nodule on sun-exposed skin; its features form the acronym AEIOU — Asymptomatic, Expanding rapidly, Immunosuppression, Older than 50, UV-exposed site — and an oncogenic Merkel cell polyomavirus is present in 80% of tumors.
MCC has high metastatic potential (sentinel-node positivity ~25%, mortality ~33% at 3 years), and survival depends on stage (90% cured with local disease, 52% with nodal, 10% with distant).
Early-stage MCC is treated with wide local excision + SLNB and often adjuvant radiation (MCC is radiosensitive); for metastatic disease, immunotherapy (avelumab/anti-PD-L1, ~33% response with durable responses; pembrolizumab >50%) has supplanted chemotherapy.
keyPoints: AEIOU: Asymptomatic, Expanding rapidly, Immunosuppression, Older than 50, UV-exposed; MCPyV in 80%
Aggressive: SLN+ ~25%, ~33% 3-year mortality; survival 90%/52%/10% for local/nodal/distant
Early: wide excision + SLNB ± adjuvant radiation (radiosensitive); metastatic: avelumab/pembrolizumab
items.reference: Merkel Cell Carcinoma: "The common clinical features of MCC can be summarized by the acronym AEIOU: asymptomatic/nontender, expand rapidly, immune suppression, older than 50 years, and ultraviolet-exposed site."

items.question: In the ABCDE rule for melanoma, what does 'E' stand for?
options: Evolving (change in size, shape, color, elevation, or new symptoms)
items.explanation: 'E' stands for Evolving — any change in size, shape, color, or elevation, or new symptoms such as bleeding, itching, or crusting. The full rule is Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variegation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolving.
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS: "evolving (any change in size, shape, color, or elevation or new symptoms such as bleeding, itching, and crusting)."

items.question: Germline mutations in which gene account for the majority of hereditary melanoma and also increase pancreatic cancer risk?
items.explanation: CDKN2A (chromosome 9p21) is the major familial melanoma gene; ~20–40% of hereditary melanomas are due to germline CDKN2A mutations, and such families also have increased pancreatic cancer risk. It encodes p16 (RB pathway) and ARF (p53 pathway).
items.reference: GENETIC SUSCEPTIBILITY TO MELANOMA: "Several studies have shown an increased risk of pancreatic cancer among melanoma-prone families with CDKN2A mutations."

items.question: What is the preferred biopsy technique for a suspicious pigmented lesion?
options: Wide excision with 2 cm margins immediately
items.explanation: An excisional biopsy with 1–3 mm (narrow) margins is preferred — it permits accurate Breslow thickness measurement and is definitive if benign. Incisional biopsy is acceptable for large or anatomically difficult lesions and does not facilitate spread.
items.reference: Biopsy: "An excisional biopsy with 1- to 3-mm margins (narrow-margin excision) is suggested."

items.question: What is the single best predictor of recurrence in newly diagnosed cutaneous melanoma?
items.explanation: Breslow thickness is the best predictor of recurrence, followed by ulceration; together they constitute the T stage. Breslow thickness is measured from the top of the granular layer (or ulcer base) to the bottom of the tumor.
items.reference: PROGNOSTIC FACTORS: "The best predictor of recurrence is Breslow thickness, followed by ulceration..."

items.question: The most common BRAF mutation in cutaneous melanoma is:
items.explanation: The most common BRAF mutation is V600E, a valine-to-glutamate substitution; BRAF is mutated in 40–50% of cutaneous melanomas, and V600E is more common in younger patients and intermittently sun-exposed skin.
items.reference: CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOGENESIS: "The BRAF mutation is most commonly a T→A point mutation that results in a valine-to-glutamate amino acid substitution (V600E)."

items.question: What radial excision margin is recommended for an invasive cutaneous melanoma that is 1.5 mm thick?
items.explanation: NCCN recommends 1–2 cm radial margins for melanomas >1.01–2 mm thick (1 cm for ≤1 mm; 2 cm for >2 mm; 0.5–1 cm for in situ). A 1.5 mm melanoma falls in the 1–2 cm category.
items.reference: MANAGEMENT OF CLINICALLY LOCALIZED MELANOMA: "invasive up to 1 mm thick, 1 cm; >1.01–2 mm, 1–2 cm; and >2 mm, 2 cm."

items.question: Sentinel lymph node biopsy is recommended (≥10% nodal risk) for which melanoma?
options: A 0.5 mm melanoma without high-risk features
A 2 mm thick (T2) melanoma, or a T1 melanoma with ulceration (T1b)
items.explanation: NCCN recommends SLNB for a ≥10% chance of nodal tumor — tumors >1 mm thick (T2) or ulcerated T1 tumors (T1b). SLNB is not recommended at ≤5% risk (≤0.75 mm without high-risk features).
items.reference: MANAGEMENT OF CLINICALLY LOCALIZED MELANOMA: "This includes patients with tumors >1 mm thick (T2) or T1 tumors that have ulceration (T1b)."

items.question: A patient has a positive sentinel lymph node but no distant disease. Why is routine completion lymphadenectomy no longer recommended?
options: It increases the risk of distant metastases
It improves relapse-free but not overall survival, at the cost of dissection morbidity
items.explanation: Completion lymphadenectomy after a positive sentinel node improves relapse-free but not overall survival, so it is no longer routine; patients instead undergo nodal-bed surveillance and are offered adjuvant therapy.
items.reference: MANAGEMENT OF CLINICALLY LOCALIZED MELANOMA: "Complete lymphadenectomy following identification of a positive sentinel lymph node improves relapse-free but not overall survival..."

items.question: What is now standard adjuvant therapy for stage IIB or IIC melanoma after wide excision and negative SLNB?
options: One year of anti-PD-1 immunotherapy (pembrolizumab or nivolumab)
items.explanation: Adjuvant anti-PD-1 immunotherapy (pembrolizumab or nivolumab) for 1 year significantly reduces recurrence or death in stage IIB/IIC melanoma and has become standard of care.
items.reference: MANAGEMENT OF CLINICALLY LOCALIZED MELANOMA: "Adjuvant anti-PD-1 immunotherapy using pembrolizumab or nivolumab for 1 year significantly reduces the risk of melanoma recurrence or death in stage IIB or IIC melanoma..."

items.question: Combination ipilimumab plus nivolumab for advanced melanoma achieves an objective response rate of approximately:
items.explanation: Combined ipilimumab + nivolumab is associated with an objective response rate of about 56% and enhanced survival compared with ipilimumab monotherapy.
items.reference: IMMUNOTHERAPY: "is associated with an objective response rate of 56% and enhanced survival compared to ipilimumab monotherapy."

items.question: In melanoma, which biomarker is the more robust predictor of response to checkpoint immunotherapy?
items.explanation: Tumor mutational burden correlates with higher response rates and longer progression-free survival on checkpoint therapy and appears to be a more robust predictor than PD-L1 expression in melanoma.
items.reference: IMMUNOTHERAPY: "TMB appears to be a more robust predictor of response compared to PD-L1 expression in melanoma."

items.question: Which statement about managing immune-related adverse events from checkpoint inhibitors is correct?
options: Glucocorticoids used to treat toxicity substantially reduce antitumor efficacy
Using immunosuppressants to mitigate toxicity does not diminish antitumor activity
irAEs only occur with anti-PD-1 monotherapy
Steroids are contraindicated for immune-related colitis
items.explanation: Using immunosuppressive agents (steroids, anti-TNF, mycophenolate) to mitigate toxicity does not diminish antitumor activity, and benefit persists even in patients who must stop immunotherapy. Toxicity is greatest with combined anti-CTLA-4 + anti-PD-1.
items.reference: IMMUNOTHERAPY: "Using immunosuppressive agents to mitigate toxicity does not diminish antitumor activity..."

items.question: Why is a MEK inhibitor combined with a BRAF inhibitor for BRAF V600-mutant melanoma?
options: MEK inhibitors are less toxic alone
To counter rapid MAP kinase pathway reactivation that drives adaptation and resistance
BRAF inhibitors are inactive without MEK inhibitors
items.explanation: Combined BRAF + MEK inhibition addresses the rapid adaptation of melanomas that reactivate the MAP kinase pathway when BRAF alone is inhibited, improving progression-free and overall survival over monotherapy.
items.reference: TARGETED THERAPY: "to address the rapid adaptation of melanomas that use MAP kinase pathway reactivation to facilitate growth when BRAF is inhibited."

items.question: A class-specific side effect of BRAF inhibitor monotherapy (occurring in up to 25% of patients) is:
options: Well-differentiated squamous cell skin cancers and keratoacanthomas
items.explanation: BRAF inhibitor monotherapy causes hyperproliferative skin lesions, including squamous cell cancers/keratoacanthomas, via paradoxical MAP kinase activation in BRAF wild-type cells. Adding a MEK inhibitor blocks this; the lesions don't metastasize and therapy can continue after excision.
items.reference: TARGETED THERAPY: "the development of hyperproliferative skin lesions, including well-differentiated squamous cell skin cancers (SCCs) in up to 25% of patients."

items.question: For a patient with BRAF-mutant metastatic melanoma, which treatment sequence is favored by survival data?
options: BRAF/MEK targeted therapy first, then immunotherapy
Immunotherapy (checkpoint blockade) first, then BRAF/MEK targeted therapy at progression
items.explanation: A randomized study showed immunotherapy (anti-CTLA-4 + anti-PD-1) followed by BRAF/MEK targeted therapy at progression conferred better overall survival than the reverse sequence, so survival data favor initial combined checkpoint immunotherapy.
items.reference: INITIAL APPROACH TO PATIENT WITH METASTATIC DISEASE: "immunotherapy followed by targeted therapy conferred statistically significant better overall survival at 2 years..."

items.question: Solid-organ transplant recipients on chronic immunosuppression have approximately how much increased risk of squamous cell carcinoma?
items.explanation: Chronically immunosuppressed solid-organ transplant recipients have a 65-fold increase in SCC (and 10-fold in BCC), and their SCCs are particularly aggressive with higher recurrence, metastasis, and mortality.
items.reference: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY: "chronically immunosuppressed solid organ transplant recipients have a 65-fold increase in SCC and a 10-fold increase in BCC."

items.question: The hedgehog pathway inhibitors vismodegib and sonidegib are indicated for:
options: Early superficial BCC as first-line therapy
Metastatic or advanced BCC not amenable to surgery or radiation
items.explanation: Hedgehog pathway inhibitors (vismodegib, sonidegib) are indicated for metastatic or advanced BCC that recurred after local therapy and is not a surgical/radiation candidate; they induce regression in ~50% but are not curative. Cemiplimab can follow progression.
items.reference: BASAL CELL CARCINOMA (Treatment): "Systemic therapy with a targeted hedgehog pathway inhibitor, such as vismodegib or sonidegib, is indicated for patients with metastatic or advanced BCC..."

items.question: Mohs micrographic surgery is the preferred modality for basal cell carcinoma in which situation?
options: Small superficial BCC on the trunk
Recurrent or high-risk lesions in cosmetically sensitive locations (eyelids, lips, nose, digits)
items.explanation: Mohs micrographic surgery (cure rates >98%) is preferred for recurrent lesions and those in high-risk or cosmetically sensitive locations where maximal tissue conservation is critical (eyelids, lips, ears, nose, digits).
items.reference: BASAL CELL CARCINOMA (Treatment): "It is the preferred modality for lesions that are recurrent, in high-risk or cosmetically sensitive locations..."

items.question: Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma at which site has a notably higher rate of regional metastasis?
items.explanation: SCC on the lower lip and ear develops regional metastases in 13% and 11% of patients, respectively — higher than SCC on sun-damaged skin elsewhere; SCC in scars, chronic ulcers, mucosa, recurrent SCC, and immunosuppressed patients also behaves aggressively.
items.reference: Squamous Cell Carcinoma (Natural History): "Tumors occurring on the lower lip and ear develop regional metastases in 13 and 11% of patients, respectively..."

items.question: An oncogenic virus present in about 80% of Merkel cell carcinomas is:
items.explanation: An oncogenic Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) is present in 80% of Merkel cell carcinomas; in virus-positive tumors there is inactivation of p53 and Rb, and viral large T antigen is expressed. MCPyV serum titers can be used for surveillance.
items.reference: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY: "an oncogenic Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) is present in 80% of tumors."

items.statement: Melanoma accounts for over half of the deaths resulting from skin cancer.
items.explanation: True — melanoma accounts for over half of skin-cancer deaths, although recent immunotherapy and targeted therapy have significantly improved survival.
items.reference: MELANOMA: "Melanoma accounts for over half of the deaths resulting from skin cancer..."

items.statement: Incisional biopsy of a melanoma facilitates spread of the tumor and should be avoided.
items.explanation: False — incisional biopsy does not appear to facilitate the spread of melanoma and is acceptable for large lesions or anatomically difficult sites where excisional biopsy is not feasible.
items.reference: Biopsy: "Incisional biopsy does not appear to facilitate the spread of melanoma."

items.statement: Cutaneous melanomas have one of the highest somatic mutation rates of all cancers, with most showing a UV-radiation mutational signature.
items.explanation: True — cutaneous melanomas have >10 mutations/Mb (one of the highest rates), and the majority (76% of primaries, 84% of metastases) show mutations indicative of UVR exposure.
items.reference: CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOGENESIS (Genomic): "Cutaneous melanomas have one of the highest somatic mutation rates (>10 mutations/Mb) among all cancers..."

items.statement: Most patients with melanoma present with disease confined to the skin and do not require staging imaging.
items.explanation: True — over 80% of patients present with disease confined to the skin and a negative history/examination, in which case imaging is not indicated (imaging is reserved for symptoms, abnormal labs, or very-high-risk primaries).
items.reference: STAGING: ">80% of patients at presentation will have disease confined to the skin and a negative history and physical examination, in which case imaging is not indicated."

items.statement: The major limitation of BRAF/MEK inhibitor therapy in melanoma is the acquisition of resistance.
items.explanation: True — although some patients achieve long disease control, the major limitation of both monotherapy and combined BRAF/MEK therapy is acquired resistance, usually via MAPK pathway reactivation.
items.reference: TARGETED THERAPY: "the major limitation of both monotherapy and combined therapy appears to be the acquisition of resistance."

items.statement: Activating c-kit mutations are more common in mucosal and acral lentiginous melanoma and may respond to imatinib.
items.explanation: True — c-kit mutations are more common in mucosal and acral lentiginous subtypes; when present, imatinib can achieve meaningful (though usually transient) responses, with an objective response rate of ~29%.
items.reference: TARGETED THERAPY: "Activating mutations in the c-kit receptor tyrosine kinase...are more common in mucosal and acral lentiginous subtypes. When activating mutations of c-kit are present, imatinib therapy can achieve clinically meaningful responses..."

items.statement: Non-melanoma skin cancers (mostly SCC and BCC) are the most common cancers in the United States.
items.explanation: True — NMSCs are the most common cancers in the US (estimated >5.3 million cases per year), with SCC ~80% and BCC ~18% (per the chapter's NMSC accounting).
items.reference: NONMELANOMA SKIN CANCERS: "NMSCs (mostly SCCs and basal cell cancer [BCC]) are the most common cancers in the United States."

items.statement: Basal cell carcinoma has a high metastatic potential in immunocompetent patients.
items.explanation: False — the metastatic potential of BCC is low (0.1%) in immunocompetent patients, though the risk of recurrence or a new primary NMSC is about 40% over 5 years.
items.reference: Basal Cell Carcinoma (Natural History): "The metastatic potential of BCC is low (0.1%) in immunocompetent patients..."

items.statement: Because keratoacanthomas can progress to metastatic SCC, they should be treated like other cutaneous squamous cell carcinomas.
items.explanation: True — although some keratoacanthomas regress spontaneously, progression to metastatic SCC has been documented, so they should be treated like other cutaneous SCCs. They occur in 15–25% of patients on BRAF inhibitor monotherapy.
items.reference: Squamous Cell Carcinoma: "because progression to metastatic SCC has been documented, KAs should be treated in the same manner as other types of cutaneous SCC."

items.statement: Merkel cell carcinoma is sensitive to radiation, PD-1-directed immunotherapy, and platinum-based chemotherapy.
items.explanation: True — like small-cell lung cancer, MCC is sensitive to radiation, PD-1-directed immunotherapy, and platinum-based chemotherapy; immunotherapy (avelumab, pembrolizumab) has supplanted chemotherapy for metastatic disease.
items.reference: MERKEL CELL CARCINOMA: "Like SCLCs, MCC is sensitive to radiation, PD-1-directed immunotherapy, and platinum-based chemotherapy."

items.statement: Broad-spectrum sunscreens with an SPF of at least 30 and physical blockers (zinc oxide, titanium dioxide) are recommended for skin-cancer prevention.
items.explanation: True — regular broad-spectrum sunscreens (SPF ≥30) and protective clothing are encouraged; physical blockers like zinc oxide and titanium dioxide are less likely to be absorbed or cause allergy than chemical sunscreens.
items.reference: PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION: "Regular use of broad-spectrum sunscreens that block UV-A and UV-B with a sun protection factor (SPF) of at least 30 and protective clothing should be encouraged."

items.statement: Nicotinamide 500 mg twice daily can reduce the development of new actinic keratoses and squamous cell carcinomas.
items.explanation: True — nicotinamide 500 mg BID may be used in patients with large numbers of actinic keratoses and SCCs to reduce the development and/or progression of disease.
items.reference: PREVENTION (NMSC): "Nicotinamide 500 mg BID may be used in patients with large numbers of actinic keratoses and SCCs to reduce the development and/or progression of disease."

items.assertion: Loss of CDKN2A function predisposes strongly to melanoma.
items.reason: CDKN2A encodes p16 and ARF, so its loss inactivates both the RB and p53 tumor-suppressor pathways that control cell-cycle entry.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because CDKN2A loss simultaneously disables the RB (via p16) and p53 (via ARF) pathways, it strongly predisposes to melanoma.
items.reference: GENETIC SUSCEPTIBILITY TO MELANOMA: "The loss of CDKN2A results in inactivation of two critical tumor-suppressor pathways, RB and p53..."

items.assertion: Routine completion lymphadenectomy is recommended for all patients with a positive sentinel lymph node.
items.reason: Completion lymphadenectomy improves overall survival in these patients.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. Completion lymphadenectomy is no longer routine, and it improves relapse-free but NOT overall survival. Both false = option 4.
items.reference: MANAGEMENT OF CLINICALLY LOCALIZED MELANOMA: "Complete lymphadenectomy following identification of a positive sentinel lymph node improves relapse-free but not overall survival, and therefore, it is no longer offered routinely."

items.assertion: BRAF inhibitors are combined with MEK inhibitors rather than used alone in BRAF-mutant melanoma.
items.reason: Melanomas rapidly adapt by reactivating the MAP kinase pathway when BRAF is inhibited alone.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because MAP kinase reactivation undermines BRAF monotherapy, adding a MEK inhibitor (downstream) improves and prolongs the response.
items.reference: TARGETED THERAPY: "to address the rapid adaptation of melanomas that use MAP kinase pathway reactivation to facilitate growth when BRAF is inhibited."

items.assertion: Squamous cell skin cancers that develop during BRAF inhibitor therapy require permanent discontinuation of the BRAF inhibitor.
items.reason: These treatment-induced squamous cell cancers have a high rate of metastasis.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. Treatment-induced SCCs have not been reported to metastasize, and the BRAF (and MEK) inhibitors can be continued safely after simple excision. Both false = option 4.
items.reference: TARGETED THERAPY: "Metastases from treatment-induced SCCs have not been reported, and BRAF and MEK inhibitors can be continued safely following simple excision of the SCCs."

items.assertion: Glucocorticoids should be used promptly for severe immune-related toxicity from checkpoint inhibitors.
items.reason: Using immunosuppressive agents to control toxicity does not diminish the antitumor activity of the immunotherapy.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because immunosuppression for toxicity does not reduce efficacy, steroids can be used promptly and confidently to prevent organ damage.
items.reference: IMMUNOTHERAPY: "Using immunosuppressive agents to mitigate toxicity does not diminish antitumor activity, and benefit is manifest even in patients who must discontinue immunotherapy due to immune-mediated toxicity."

items.assertion: A BRAF mutation test should be obtained when melanoma is first diagnosed as stage IV.
items.reason: Treatment algorithms for metastatic melanoma start with determining the tumor's BRAF status.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because management branches on BRAF status (immunotherapy for wild-type; immunotherapy then BRAF/MEK for mutant), BRAF testing is obtained at stage IV diagnosis.
items.reference: INITIAL APPROACH TO PATIENT WITH METASTATIC DISEASE: "Treatment algorithms start with determining the melanoma's BRAF status."

items.assertion: Squamous cell carcinomas in solid-organ transplant recipients behave more aggressively than in immunocompetent patients.
items.reason: Chronic immunosuppression greatly increases SCC incidence and is associated with higher rates of local recurrence, metastasis, and mortality.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: chronic immunosuppression both raises SCC incidence (65-fold) and makes the SCCs more aggressive, with higher recurrence/metastasis/mortality.
items.reference: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY: "SCCs in this population are particularly aggressive, demonstrating higher rates of local recurrence, metastasis, and mortality."

items.assertion: Mohs micrographic surgery is preferred for basal cell carcinoma on the eyelids, nose, and digits.
items.reason: Mohs maximizes tissue conservation while providing high cure rates, which is critical in cosmetically and functionally sensitive sites.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because Mohs conserves the most uninvolved tissue with >98% cure, it is preferred where tissue conservation matters most (eyelids, lips, ears, nose, digits).
items.reference: BASAL CELL CARCINOMA (Treatment): "It is the preferred modality for lesions that are recurrent, in high-risk or cosmetically sensitive locations...for which maximal tissue conservation is critical (e.g., the eyelids, lips, ears, nose, and digits)."

items.assertion: Merkel cell carcinoma should be regarded as an aggressive cancer requiring nodal staging.
items.reason: MCC has a high probability of spread to regional lymph nodes, with sentinel-node positivity rates around 25%.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because MCC frequently spreads to regional nodes (SLN+ ~25%) with substantial mortality, it is treated aggressively with nodal staging (SLNB).
items.reference: Merkel Cell Carcinoma (Natural History): "MCCs usually present locally yet have a high probability of spread to regional lymph nodes and distant sites."

items.assertion: Avoiding sunburns is an important melanoma-prevention measure, especially in childhood.
items.reason: Early episodes of sunburn may be a greater melanoma risk than chronic tanning, and UV damage begins early even though cancers develop years later.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because early sunburns may carry greater risk than chronic tanning and UV damage starts early, sun protection (and sunburn avoidance) should begin early in life.
items.reference: PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION: "Early episodes of sunburns may be a greater risk than chronic tanning."

items.subtopic: Cancer of the Skin | MELANOMA | RISK FACTORS AND EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Understanding Melanoma Incidence and Mortality Trends
items.content: Melanoma incidence varies significantly based on skin eumelanin levels, with higher rates in populations with low skin eumelanin. The U.S. has seen a rise in melanoma cases, making it the fifth leading cancer in men and sixth in women as of 2024. Despite increasing incidence, mortality rates are decreasing due to advances in immunotherapy and targeted therapy.
keyPoints: Incidence is 1/100,000 per year in high eumelanin populations; up to 27/100,000 in low eumelanin populations.
Melanoma is the fifth leading cancer in men (59,170 new cases in 2024) and sixth in women (41,460 new cases in 2024).
Mortality rates are decreasing: 1-year relative survival rate increased from 42% (2008–2010) to 55% (2013–2015) post-approval of ipilimumab and vemurafenib.
Annual mortality rate dropped by 7% in those aged 20–64 years and 5–6% for individuals ≥65 years from 2013 to 2017.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · Cancer of the Skin | MELANOMA | RISK FACTORS AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Cancer of the Skin | MELANOMA | RISK FACTORS AND EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Risk Factors for Melanoma Development
items.content: Melanoma risk is influenced by genetic, environmental, and immunologic factors. Key risk factors include vulnerability to sun damage, abnormal melanocyte growth, and immunosuppression. These contribute to the increased likelihood of developing melanoma.
keyPoints: Risk factors: sun damage (light/red skin, photodamaged skin), history of UVR exposure, prior skin cancers.
Abnormal melanocyte growth: increased number or size of nevi, atypical mole features (multiple colors, speckles).
Immunosuppression increases risk: innate, functional, or drug-induced.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · Cancer of the Skin | MELANOMA | RISK FACTORS AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Cancer of the Skin | MELANOMA | RISK FACTORS AND EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Demographic Patterns in Melanoma Epidemiology
items.content: Melanoma incidence and mortality exhibit distinct demographic patterns. Men are more frequently affected than women, with a ratio of 1.4:1. The median age at diagnosis is 66 years, and mortality rates increase significantly after age 55, particularly in men over 65.
keyPoints: Men are slightly more affected than women (1.4:1).
Median age at diagnosis is 66 years.
Mortality rates rise after age 55, with the highest mortality in men >65 years.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · Cancer of the Skin | MELANOMA | RISK FACTORS AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Incidence and Demographics of Skin Cancer
items.content: The incidence of nonmelanoma skin cancer (NMSC) and melanoma is rising globally, with significant disparities in rates among different populations. Whites have the highest incidence of melanoma, while dark-skinned populations experience lower rates but often present at more advanced stages.
keyPoints: 2-3 million people develop NMSC annually.
In 2020, there were 324,635 cases of melanoma worldwide.
Whites have the highest incidence of melanoma: 37.9 (men) and 25.2 (women) per 100,000.
Melanoma rates in blacks and East Asians are 10-20 times lower than in whites.
Non-sun-exposed melanomas are more common in nonwhite populations.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Prognosis and Treatment Challenges of Non-Sun-Exposed Melanomas
items.content: Non-sun-exposed melanomas, such as acral and mucosal types, are more prevalent in nonwhite populations and have a poorer prognosis due to different biology and lower response rates to immunotherapy.
keyPoints: Non-sun-exposed melanomas have a higher frequency in Asian and dark-skinned populations.
These melanomas have a lower probability of responding to immunotherapy.
Mortality from non-sun-exposed melanomas is increasing, particularly noted in China.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: CDKN2A Mutations Significantly Increase Melanoma Risk
items.content: Germline mutations in the CDKN2A gene are a major genetic factor in hereditary melanomas, affecting 20–40% of such cases. These mutations lead to loss of function in two critical tumor-suppressor proteins, p16 and ARF, which control cell cycle entry by regulating RB and p53 pathways.
keyPoints: CDKN2A mutations account for 20–40% of hereditary melanomas (0.2–2% of all melanomas).
70% of cutaneous melanomas have mutations or deletions at the CDKN2A locus on chromosome 9p21.
Loss of CDKN2A inactivates RB and p53 tumor-suppressor pathways, critical for cell cycle control.
Increased risk of pancreatic cancer is observed among families with CDKN2A mutations.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · GENETIC SUSCEPTIBILITY TO MELANOMA

items.title: MC1R Gene Variants Contribute to Moderate Melanoma Risk
items.content: The MC1R gene is a moderate-risk factor for melanoma susceptibility. Its polymorphisms lead to reduced production of photoprotective eumelanin and increased pheomelanin, which contributes to higher UV sensitivity and oxidative DNA damage.
keyPoints: MC1R is associated with the red hair color phenotype, characterized by lightly colored skin, red hair, freckles, and increased sun sensitivity.
MC1R polymorphisms result in partial or full loss of signaling, leading to non-photoprotective pheomelanin production.
Increased pheomelanin contributes to melanoma risk via oxidative damage and reduced DNA repair.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · GENETIC SUSCEPTIBILITY TO MELANOMA

items.title: Sun Protection as Primary Prevention for Skin Cancer
items.content: Primary prevention of melanoma and non-melanoma skin cancer (NMSC) focuses on sun protection. Initiatives like the SunSmart program have shown that behavioral changes can reduce NMSC and melanoma incidence. Preventive measures should begin early in life due to early UV damage, with a greater risk posed by sunburns than chronic tanning.
keyPoints: Sun protection reduces incidence of NMSC and melanoma.
Behavioral change programs like SunSmart have been effective.
UV damage starts early; sunburns pose higher risk than chronic tanning.
Use broad-spectrum sunscreens with SPF ≥ 30, physical blockers preferred.
$\beta$-endorphins released after UV exposure in compulsive tanners.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION

items.title: Secondary Prevention through Education and Screening for Skin Cancer
items.content: Secondary prevention includes education and screening to detect skin cancer early. High-risk individuals, such as those with atypical moles or a personal history of melanoma, should undergo full-body skin exams by dermatologists. Genetic testing may benefit families with multiple cases of melanoma or related cancers. Early detection allows for simpler treatments with higher cure rates.
keyPoints: Education and screening are crucial for early detection in high-risk individuals.
Full-body skin exams recommended for those with atypical moles or personal history of melanoma.
Genetic testing advised for families with multiple melanomas or related cancers.
Atypical nevi should be removed with ≥ 5-mm margin.
Monthly self-screening complements provider-based screening.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION

items.title: ABCDE Criteria for Early Melanoma Detection
items.content: The ABCDE criteria are essential for early detection of melanoma, focusing on asymmetry, border irregularity, color variegation, diameter greater than 6 mm, and evolving characteristics. These features help differentiate between benign lesions and potential melanomas.
keyPoints: ABCDEs: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variegation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolving
Diameter threshold for concern is >6 mm (size of a pencil eraser)
Any atypical nevus ('ugly duckling') should be considered suspicious
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Guidelines for Biopsy and Histologic Assessment in Suspected Melanoma
items.content: Suspicious pigmented lesions, especially those exhibiting changes or meeting ABCDE criteria, should be biopsied. Excisional biopsy with 1- to 3-mm margins is preferred for accurate histologic assessment and definitive treatment if benign. For large or difficult-to-access lesions, incisional biopsy may be used without facilitating melanoma spread.
keyPoints: Excisional biopsy with 1- to 3-mm margins recommended
Incisional biopsy acceptable when excision is not feasible
Biopsy should include deepest component and base pigment
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Genomic Classification of Melanoma: Implications for Prognosis and Treatment
items.content: The classification of melanoma has evolved to incorporate genomic alterations, which are increasingly relevant in clinical practice. Genomic pathway characterization is anticipated to influence prognosis, surveillance strategies, surgical decisions, and medical therapy.
keyPoints: Genomic alteration pathways are not currently part of AJCC staging or prognostic considerations but are becoming common in clinical practice.
Characterizing genomic profiles may soon impact melanoma management.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Mutation Rates and Patterns in Cutaneous Melanomas
items.content: Cutaneous melanomas exhibit high somatic mutation rates, with a majority showing mutations indicative of UV radiation (UVR) exposure. Mutation patterns vary by body site, with sun-damaged skin harboring more mutations.
keyPoints: Somatic mutation rate in cutaneous melanomas is >10 mutations/Mb.
76% of primary tumors and 84% of metastatic melanomas show UVR-related mutations.
Melanomas from chronic sun-damaged skin have substantially more mutations.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Driver Mutations in Melanoma: Key Genes and Pathways
items.content: Specific driver mutations, such as those in BRAF, RAS, and NF1, are crucial for melanoma pathogenesis. These mutations affect key proliferative pathways like MAP kinase and PI3K/AKT.
keyPoints: BRAF mutation (V600E) is common in younger patients and intermittent sun exposure sites.
~20% of melanomas have RAS mutations, while 40–50% have BRAF mutations.
NF1 loss-of-function mutations occur in 10–15% of melanomas.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · CLASSIFICATION AND PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Key Prognostic Factors in Melanoma: Breslow Thickness and Ulceration
items.content: The prognosis of melanoma is significantly influenced by the AJCC staging classification, with Breslow thickness being the best predictor of recurrence. This measurement, along with ulceration, determines the T stage for melanoma.
keyPoints: Breslow thickness and ulceration are critical in determining the T stage.
Thicker tumors correlate with higher risk of recurrence.
Ulceration presence further worsens prognosis.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · PROGNOSTIC FACTORS

items.title: Impact of Demographics and Anatomic Site on Melanoma Prognosis
items.content: Prognosis in melanoma varies by demographic factors such as age, gender, and anatomic site. Women generally have better survival rates than men at stages I or II, possibly due to earlier detection. Older males often present with delayed diagnosis leading to worse outcomes.
keyPoints: Women have better survival rates than men in early-stage melanoma.
Older males (>60) tend to have delayed diagnoses and poorer prognosis.
Anatomic sites like the scalp, hands, feet, and mucous membranes are associated with worse outcomes.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · PROGNOSTIC FACTORS

items.title: When to Image in Melanoma Staging
items.content: Imaging is generally not indicated for patients with melanoma confined to the skin and a negative history/physical examination. However, imaging should be considered for node-negative low-stage melanomas with high-risk gene expression profiles (GEP) or very-high-risk primaries such as those >4 mm with ulceration, and clinical stages IIB and IIC due to higher chances of occult distant metastases.
keyPoints: Imaging is not indicated if disease is confined to the skin (>80% cases).
Consider imaging for node-negative low-stage melanomas with high-risk GEP.
Very-high-risk primaries (e.g., >4 mm with ulceration, clinical stages IIB and IIC) may warrant imaging due to higher risk of occult distant metastases.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · ☐ STAGING

items.title: Role of LDH in Melanoma Staging
items.content: LDH levels are part of the AJCC system for stage IV melanoma. Elevated LDH can indicate more advanced disease and should prompt further evaluation with imaging such as CT or PET scans.
keyPoints: LDH is included in the AJCC staging system for stage IV melanoma.
Elevated LDH levels suggest a need for extensive evaluation, including CT or PET scans.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · ☐ STAGING

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF CLINICALLY LOCALIZED MELANOMA (STAGE I, II)
items.title: Optimal Surgical Margins for Melanoma Excision
items.content: Surgical wide excision with appropriate radial margins is essential to minimize local recurrence in melanoma. The NCCN provides specific margin recommendations based on tumor thickness.
keyPoints: Primary MIS: 0.5–1.0 cm margin
Invasive up to 1 mm thick: 1 cm margin
>1.01–2 mm: 1–2 cm margin
>2 mm: 2 cm margin
Smaller margins for anatomically constrained areas (e.g., face, hands) may be used; Mohs surgery can be advantageous.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · MANAGEMENT OF CLINICALLY LOCALIZED MELANOMA (STAGE I, II)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF CLINICALLY LOCALIZED MELANOMA (STAGE I, II)
items.title: Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy Indications
items.content: SLNB is recommended for patients with a significant risk of nodal involvement to guide adjuvant therapy decisions. The NCCN provides specific criteria based on tumor characteristics.
keyPoints: SLNB recommended for >10% node positivity risk: Tumors >1 mm thick or T1 tumors with ulceration
Consider SLNB for 5–10% risk (e.g., tumors 0.75-1.0 mm, regressed tumors)
Not recommended for ≤5% risk: Melanomas ≤0.75 mm without high-risk features
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · MANAGEMENT OF CLINICALLY LOCALIZED MELANOMA (STAGE I, II)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF CLINICALLY LOCALIZED MELANOMA (STAGE I, II)
items.title: Adjuvant Therapy for High-Risk Stage IIB/IIC Melanoma
items.content: Patients with high-risk stage IIB or IIC melanomas benefit from adjuvant anti-PD-1 immunotherapy to reduce recurrence and mortality.
keyPoints: Stage IIB/IIC melanomas have a 13–18% 5-year probability of death post-WLE
Adjuvant therapy with pembrolizumab or nivolumab for 1 year is standard care
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · MANAGEMENT OF CLINICALLY LOCALIZED MELANOMA (STAGE I, II)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF REGIONALLY METASTATIC MELANOMA (STAGE III)
items.title: Adjuvant Therapy Post-Resection in Stage III Melanoma
items.content: After complete resection of stage III melanoma, adjuvant systemic therapy is recommended due to the high risk of developing distant metastases. Options include anti-PD-1 inhibitors (nivolumab or pembrolizumab) and BRAF/MEK inhibitors for tumors with a BRAF V600 mutation.
keyPoints: Adjuvant systemic therapy should be offered post-resection to reduce the risk of distant metastases.
Anti-PD-1 inhibitors (nivolumab or pembrolizumab) and targeted therapy with BRAF/MEK inhibitors are current options for adjuvant treatment.
Both anti-PD-1 and targeted therapies have shown benefits in disease-free and overall survival.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · MANAGEMENT OF REGIONALLY METASTATIC MELANOMA (STAGE III)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF REGIONALLY METASTATIC MELANOMA (STAGE III)
items.title: Neoadjuvant Therapy for Bulky Stage III Melanoma
items.content: For patients with bulky stage III melanoma (stages IIIC and IIID), neoadjuvant therapy followed by resection and adjuvant pembrolizumab has shown significant improvements in event-free and overall survival compared to adjuvant-only approaches.
keyPoints: Neoadjuvant therapy followed by surgery and adjuvant pembrolizumab improves outcomes for bulky stage III melanoma.
>50% probability of achieving a pathologic complete response with neoadjuvant treatment.
Low recurrence rates at 1 and 2 years post-treatment in patients receiving neoadjuvant plus adjuvant therapy.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · MANAGEMENT OF REGIONALLY METASTATIC MELANOMA (STAGE III)

items.title: Prognosis and Treatment of Stage IV Melanoma
items.content: Stage IV melanoma prognosis varies based on metastatic sites, with skin/subcutaneous (M1a) having better outcomes than lung (M1b), visceral/bone (M1c), or brain (M1d). Elevated serum LDH indicates poor prognosis and classifies patients as M1c. Recent advances in targeted therapy and immunotherapy have significantly improved survival rates, especially for M1a and M1b stages.
keyPoints: 84% of melanoma patients present with stage I or II at diagnosis; 4% have metastases initially.
15-year survival <10% before 2010; nearly 50% for M1a/M1b with new therapies.
Elevated serum LDH is a poor prognostic factor, classifying as M1c.
FDA-approved treatments include immune checkpoint inhibitors and targeted therapy agents.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · Metastatic Disease

items.title: Management of Oligometastatic Melanoma
items.content: Local treatment modalities like surgery or stereotactic radiosurgery are viable for patients with a limited number of metastases (oligometastatic disease), potentially leading to long-term disease-free survival. These treatments can be combined with systemic therapy, and surgery may also facilitate mutational analysis or TIL harvesting.
keyPoints: Local modalities recommended for oligometastatic disease; best candidates have solitary metastases.
Patients free of disease post-treatment should consider adjuvant therapy due to high risk of new metastases.
Surgery can be adjunctive to systemic therapy if few oligometastases remain.
Tumor resection may allow for mutational profiling or TIL therapy.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · Metastatic Disease

items.title: Mechanism and Efficacy of Immune Checkpoint Blockade Therapies
items.content: Immune checkpoint blockade therapies, such as anti-CTLA-4 (ipilimumab), anti-PD-1 (nivolumab, pembrolizumab), and combinations thereof, enhance antitumor activity by blocking inhibitory signals on T cells. This leads to improved proliferation, cytokine production, and cytotoxicity against tumors.
keyPoints: Ipilimumab was the first drug shown in a randomized trial to improve survival in metastatic melanoma patients.
Anti-PD-1 monotherapy or checkpoint combinations have higher objective response rates than anti-CTLA-4 monotherapy.
Combined therapy with ipilimumab and nivolumab shows an objective response rate of 56% and enhanced survival compared to ipilimumab alone.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · IMMUNOTHERAPY

items.title: Biomarkers for Predicting Response to Checkpoint Inhibitors
items.content: Tumor mutational burden (TMB) is a more robust predictor of response to checkpoint inhibitors than PD-L1 expression in melanoma. Patients with >5% PD-1 expression on T cells can benefit from nivolumab, though some without detectable PD-1 expression may still respond.
keyPoints: TMB correlates with higher probability of objective response and longer progression-free survival.
>5% PD-1 expression on T cells in melanoma biopsy samples indicates potential clinical benefit from nivolumab monotherapy.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · IMMUNOTHERAPY

items.title: Managing Toxicity in Immune Checkpoint Therapy
items.content: Immune checkpoint therapies can cause a novel spectrum of immune-related adverse events affecting various organs. The severity is highest with combined anti-CTLA-4 and anti-PD-1 therapy. Early intervention with steroids or other immunosuppressive agents can mitigate toxicity without diminishing antitumor activity.
keyPoints: Common side effects include skin rash, diarrhea, thyroiditis, hypophysitis, hepatitis, nephritis, pneumonitis, myocarditis, and neuritis.
Toxicity severity is greatest with combined anti-CTLA-4 and anti-PD-1 therapy.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · IMMUNOTHERAPY

items.title: BRAF and MEK Inhibitors in Melanoma Treatment
items.content: Approximately 50% of melanomas have mutations in the RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK pathway. BRAF inhibitors like vemurafenib, dabrafenib, and encorafenib are effective for melanomas with a mutation at position 600 in BRAF. Combining BRAF and MEK inhibitors (e.g., dabrafenib + trametinib) improves progression-free and overall survival compared to monotherapy.
keyPoints: 50% of melanomas have mutations in the RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK pathway
BRAF inhibitors approved for BRAF mutation at position 600
Combined BRAF and MEK inhibition improves progression-free and overall survival
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · TARGETED THERAPY

items.title: Resistance Mechanisms in Targeted Therapy for Melanoma
items.content: Resistance to BRAF and MEK inhibitors often involves reactivation of the MAPK pathway, typically through RAS mutations or mutant BRAF amplification. Patients who develop resistance may benefit from immunotherapy or clinical trials.
keyPoints: Resistance mechanisms include MAPK pathway reactivation
RAS mutations or mutant BRAF amplification are common causes
Patients with resistance can be candidates for immunotherapy or trials
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · TARGETED THERAPY

items.title: Side Effects and Management of BRAF Inhibitors
items.content: BRAF inhibitor monotherapy can lead to hyperproliferative skin lesions in up to 25% of patients, which are rare during combined therapy with MEK inhibitors. Cardiac and ocular toxicities require medical evaluation and may necessitate discontinuation of therapy.
keyPoints: Hyperproliferative skin lesions occur in up to 25% of patients on BRAF monotherapy
Cardiac and ocular toxicities are infrequent but serious
MEK inhibitors reduce the incidence of hyperproliferative skin lesions
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · TARGETED THERAPY

items.subtopic: INITIAL APPROACH TO PATIENT WITH METASTATIC DISEASE | FOLLOW-UP AND SURVIVORSHIP
items.title: Importance of BRAF Status in Treatment Decisions for Metastatic Melanoma
items.content: Upon diagnosis of stage IV melanoma, molecular testing to determine the presence of BRAF or c-kit mutations is crucial. The treatment approach depends on the BRAF status: immunotherapy is recommended for BRAF wild-type tumors, while a combination of checkpoint immunotherapy followed by targeted therapy (BRAF/MEK) at progression has shown better overall survival in BRAF-mutated melanomas.
keyPoints: Molecular testing for BRAF or c-kit mutations should be conducted on any tumor sample, with preference given to metastatic lesion biopsies due to minimal discordance between primary and metastatic lesions.
Treatment algorithms start by determining the melanoma's BRAF status.
For BRAF wild-type tumors, immunotherapy is recommended.

In BRAF-mutated melanomas, a randomized study showed that anti-CTLA-4 plus anti-PD-1 followed by BRAF/MEK targeted therapy at progression conferred statistically significant better overall survival at 2 years and a trend toward better progression-free survival compared to the opposite sequence.
Survival data favor initial use of combined checkpoint immunotherapy in advanced disease settings.
Enrollment in clinical trials is an important consideration for patients with metastatic melanoma.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · INITIAL APPROACH TO PATIENT WITH METASTATIC DISEASE | FOLLOW-UP AND SURVIVORSHIP

items.subtopic: INITIAL APPROACH TO PATIENT WITH METASTATIC DISEASE | FOLLOW-UP AND SURVIVORSHIP
items.title: Guidelines for Surveillance and Survivorship in Melanoma Patients
items.content: Annual skin examinations are recommended for all melanoma patients. Routine blood work and imaging are not advised for stages IA-IIA unless symptomatic, while surveillance diagnostic imaging may be considered for stages IIB–III with signs or symptoms of recurrence. The NCCN does not recommend surveillance imaging for asymptomatic patients free of disease 5+ years post-treatment.
keyPoints: Annual skin examinations are recommended for all melanoma survivors.
Routine blood work and imaging are not recommended for stages IA-IIA unless symptomatic.
Surveillance diagnostic imaging may be considered for stages IIB–III with signs or symptoms of recurrence.
The NCCN does not recommend surveillance imaging in asymptomatic patients free of disease 5+ years post-treatment.
Advocacy and survivorship focus on genetic screening, sun awareness, and managing chronic treatment-related side effects such as lymphedema, neuropathies, and gastrointestinal/endocrine disorders.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · INITIAL APPROACH TO PATIENT WITH METASTATIC DISEASE | FOLLOW-UP AND SURVIVORSHIP

items.subtopic: NONMELANOMA SKIN CANCERS | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY
items.title: Incidence and Metastatic Potential of Nonmelanoma Skin Cancers (NMSCs)
items.content: NMSCs, primarily SCCs and BCCs, are the most common cancers in the United States with an estimated annual incidence exceeding 5.3 million cases. SCCs account for 80% while BCCs make up 18%. Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC), though less common, has seen a significant increase in incidence, tripling over the past two decades with an 8% annual rise. MCC metastasizes more frequently than other NMSCs, with sentinel lymph node positivity rates of 25%, compared to 12-19% for melanoma, and mortality rates approaching 33% at three years.
keyPoints: Annual incidence of NMSCs in the U.S. is over 5.3 million cases
SCCs account for 80% and BCCs for 18% of NMSCs
MCC incidence has tripled over 20 years with an annual increase of 8%
Sentinel lymph node positivity rates: MCC at 25%, melanoma 12-19%
3-year mortality rate for MCC is approximately 33%
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · NONMELANOMA SKIN CANCERS | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: NONMELANOMA SKIN CANCERS | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY
items.title: Risk Factors and Pathogenesis of NMSCs
items.content: UVR exposure, including tanning bed use, is a significant cause of NMSCs. As few as four annual tanning bed visits increase BCC risk by 15% and SCC risk by 11%. Other risk factors include cigarette smoking (especially for lip or oral SCC), inherited DNA repair disorders like xeroderma pigmentosum, and immunosuppression, which increases SCC and BCC incidence significantly in solid organ transplant recipients. Genetic mutations such as p53 and N-RAS are linked to UV damage in SCCs, while BCC is associated with hedgehog signaling pathway gene damage.
keyPoints: UVR exposure is a major cause of NMSCs
Four tanning bed visits per year increase BCC risk by 15% and SCC risk by 11%
Cigarette smoking increases lip/oral SCC risk
Immunosuppression leads to a 65-fold increase in SCC and 10-fold increase in BCC among transplant recipients
SCCs linked with p53 and N-RAS mutations; BCCs linked with hedgehog pathway gene damage
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · NONMELANOMA SKIN CANCERS | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: NONMELANOMA SKIN CANCERS | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY
items.title: Viral Etiology and Genetic Factors in Merkel Cell Carcinoma (MCC)
items.content: While MCC is related to UV exposure, age, and immunosuppression, it also has a viral etiology with Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) present in 80% of tumors. In MCPyV-positive MCCs, tumor-suppressor genes like p53 and retinoblastoma protein are inactivated, and the viral large T antigen is expressed on tumor cells. Despite detectable immune responses to polyoma viral proteins, this response is insufficient for malignancy eradication.
keyPoints: MCPyV present in 80% of MCCs
Inactivation of p53 and retinoblastoma protein in MCPyV-positive tumors
Expression of viral large T antigen on tumor cells
Insufficient immune response to eradicate MCPyV-associated malignancy
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · NONMELANOMA SKIN CANCERS | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY

items.title: Recognizing Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) Variants
items.content: Basal Cell Carcinoma can present in various forms, each with distinct clinical features. Superficial BCC appears as erythematous scaling plaques and may be confused with benign dermatoses. Nodular BCC presents as a pearly nodule with telangiectatic vessels, which might resemble melanoma if pigmented. The most aggressive subtypes like morpheaform and micronodular BCC present as indurated plaques with indistinct borders.
keyPoints: Superficial BCC: erythematous scaling plaques on trunk and proximal extremities, can mimic nummular eczema or psoriasis.
Nodular BCC: pearly nodules with telangiectatic vessels, may appear pigmented leading to confusion with melanoma.
Morpheaform/infiltrative/micronodular BCC: indurated plaques with indistinct borders, often more extensive than clinically apparent.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Identifying Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) and Keratoacanthoma
items.content: Primary cutaneous SCC manifests as ulcerated nodules or erosions, often mistaken for warts. It can metastasize and has hallmark dotted/coiled vessels on dermatoscopy.
