Keratoacanthomas, a low-grade form of SCC, appear as dome-shaped papules with central craters and should be treated like other SCCs due to potential progression.
keyPoints: SCC: ulcerated nodules or erosions on sun-exposed skin, can mimic warts, has dotted/coiled vessels under dermatoscopy.
Keratoacanthoma: dome-shaped papule with central keratotic crater, occurs in 15–25% of patients on BRAF inhibitors.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Understanding Merkel Cell Carcinoma (MCC) Presentation
items.content: Merkel Cell Carcinoma typically presents as raised, flesh-colored nodules that can rapidly enlarge. It is most common on sun-exposed areas and in older individuals with immune suppression. The AEIOU acronym helps remember its clinical features: Asymptomatic/nontender, Expanding rapidly, Immune suppression, Older than 50 years, Ultraviolet exposure.
keyPoints: MCC: raised nodules or masses, often flesh-colored, found on sun-exposed areas like head/neck/extremities.
AEIOU mnemonic: Asymptomatic/nontender, Expanding rapidly, Immune suppression, Older than 50 years, Ultraviolet exposure.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Basal Cell Carcinoma: Risk Factors for Aggression and Recurrence
items.content: The natural history of BCC involves slow enlargement with local invasiveness. The risk of recurrence or a new primary NMSC is about 40% over 5 years, influenced by tumor size, duration, location, and subtype. Larger lesions and certain subtypes like micronodular, infiltrative, and morpheaform are more aggressive.
keyPoints: BCC has low metastatic potential (0.1%) in immunocompetent patients.
Risk of recurrence or new primary NMSC is about 40% over 5 years.
Higher risk with central face, ears, or scalp location.
Small nodular, pigmented, cystic, or superficial BCCs respond well to treatment.
Large lesions and aggressive subtypes (micronodular, infiltrative, morpheaform) are more challenging.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · NATURAL HISTORY

items.title: Merkel Cell Carcinoma: Prognostic Factors and Survival Rates
items.content: MCC often spreads to regional lymph nodes and distant sites. Survival rates depend on disease extent: 90% cure rate for local disease, 52% with nodal involvement, and 10% with distant disease. Prognosis improves with viral presence, RB protein expression, and CD8+ T cell infiltration.
keyPoints: MCC spreads to regional lymph nodes and distant sites.
Survival rates: 90% for local disease, 52% with nodal involvement, 10% with distant disease.
Prognosis improved by viral presence, RB protein expression, CD8+ T cell infiltration.
Worse prognosis with p63 expression, lymphovascular pattern, and immunosuppression.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · NATURAL HISTORY

items.title: Choosing the Appropriate Treatment for Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC)
items.content: The treatment of BCC varies based on tumor characteristics, patient factors, and location. Superficial nodular BCCs are often treated with electrodesiccation and curettage (ED&C), while more aggressive or recurrent tumors in sensitive areas may require Mohs micrographic surgery (MMS) for its high cure rate and tissue conservation.
keyPoints: ED&C is most frequent for superficial, minimally invasive nodular BCCs and low-risk tumors.
Wide local excision is used for more aggressive subtypes or cosmetic reasons.
MMS has a >98% cure rate and is preferred for recurrent lesions in high-risk or cosmetically sensitive locations.
RT can be an option for non-surgical candidates or as adjunct therapy for high-risk tumors.
Imiquimod treats superficial and smaller nodular BCCs but isn't FDA approved for the latter.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · BASAL CELL CARCINOMA

items.title: Advanced Treatment Options for Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC)
items.content: For metastatic or advanced BCC, targeted therapy with hedgehog pathway inhibitors like vismodegib or sonidegib is used when surgery or RT are not options. These treatments do not cure but can induce regression in about 50% of patients, with a median response duration of 9–12 months for metastatic disease and ~2 years for locally advanced disease.
keyPoints: Targeted therapy induces regression in approximately 50% of patients.
Median duration of response is 9–12 months for metastatic disease and ~2 years for locally advanced disease.
Checkpoint immunotherapy with cemiplimab can be used after progression on targeted therapy.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · BASAL CELL CARCINOMA

items.subtopic: SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA | MERKEL CELL CARCINOMA
items.title: Systemic Therapy and Neoadjuvant Use in SCC
items.content: Cemiplimab, a PD-1 inhibitor, is the systemic therapy of choice for advanced squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), with 47% tumor regression. It's also used neoadjuvantly in stages II to IV, showing over 50% probability of pathologic complete response, especially beneficial for high-risk or technically challenging cases.
keyPoints: Cemiplimab induces tumor regression in 47% of patients with advanced SCC.
Neoadjuvant cemiplimab is associated with >50% probability of pathologic complete response.
Used as standard care in very-high-risk or technically difficult to resect cases.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA | MERKEL CELL CARCINOMA

items.subtopic: SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA | MERKEL CELL CARCINOMA
items.title: Immunotherapy and Follow-up in Merkel Cell Carcinoma (MCC)
items.content: For metastatic MCC, immunotherapy is preferred over chemotherapy. Avelumab shows a 33% objective response rate with 82% durability; pembrolizumab achieves >50% response in advanced cases, with median duration nearing 2 years. Follow-up includes regular skin and nodal exams, serum antibody titers to MCPyV for relapse monitoring, and imaging if titers remain elevated post-treatment.
keyPoints: Avelumab leads to objective responses in 33% of advanced MCC patients with 82% durability.
Pembrolizumab achieves >50% response rate in advanced MCC, median duration ~2 years.
Follow-up involves skin exams, nodal basin checks, and MCPyV antibody titers for relapse monitoring.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA | MERKEL CELL CARCINOMA

items.title: Strategies for Preventing Nonmelanoma Skin Cancer (NMSC)
items.content: Preventive strategies for NMSC include active surveillance, chemoprophylaxis, and field therapy. Patients on immunosuppressive medications or BRAF-targeted therapy should be actively monitored. Chemoprophylaxis with synthetic retinoids and reducing immunosuppression can help manage multiple tumors. Nicotinamide 500 mg BID is recommended for patients with numerous actinic keratoses and SCCs to reduce disease progression.
keyPoints: Active surveillance for patients on immunosuppressive medications or BRAF-targeted therapy
Chemoprophylaxis using synthetic retinoids and reducing immunosuppression when possible
Nicotinamide 500 mg BID for patients with numerous actinic keratoses and SCCs
Field therapy options: topical 5-fluorouracil (with or without calcipotriol), ingenol mebutate, imiquimod
Manage older, immunosuppressed patients with the lowest doses of immunosuppression possible
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · ☐ PREVENTION | OTHER NONMELANOMA CUTANEOUS MALIGNANCIES

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Other Nonmelanoma Skin Cancers (NMSCs)
items.content: Other NMSCs include neoplasms of cutaneous adnexae, sarcomas, and lymphomas. These account for 1–2% of all NMSCs. Lymphomas can mimic benign conditions like psoriasis and eczema. Extramammary Paget's disease is an uncommon apocrine malignancy arising from epidermal stem cells.
keyPoints: Neoplasms of cutaneous adnexae and sarcomas account for 1–2% of NMSCs
Lymphomas (B- or T-cell origin) can manifest in the skin, mimicking psoriasis and eczema
Extramammary Paget's disease is an uncommon apocrine malignancy from epidermal stem cells
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · ☐ PREVENTION | OTHER NONMELANOMA CUTANEOUS MALIGNANCIES

items.reason: A multidisciplinary approach involving endocrinology and rheumatology helps manage potential immune-related adverse events.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why checkpoint immunotherapy can be safely administered.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · IMMUNOTHERAPY

items.assertion: Using PD-1 expression to select therapy is straightforward and effective.
items.reason: Some patients without detectable PD-1 expression still respond to immunotherapy, making it problematic.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because using PD-1 expression for selecting therapy remains problematic.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · IMMUNOTHERAPY

items.assertion: The major limitation of both monotherapy and combined therapy with BRAF and MEK inhibitors is the acquisition of resistance.
items.reason: Resistance mechanisms often involve reactivation of the MAPK pathway through RAS mutations or mutant BRAF amplification.
items.explanation: Both the assertion about resistance being a major limitation and the reason involving MAPK pathway reactivation are true, with the latter explaining the former.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · TARGETED THERAPY

items.assertion: Tebentafusp is effective for cutaneous melanoma.
items.reason: Tebentafusp targets gp100 on melanoma and CD3 on T cells, leading to improved outcomes in metastatic uveal melanoma patients with HLA-A*02.01.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because tebentafusp is specifically approved for uveal melanoma, not cutaneous melanoma.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · TARGETED THERAPY

items.subtopic: INITIAL APPROACH TO PATIENT WITH METASTATIC DISEASE | FOLLOW-UP AND SURVIVORSHIP
items.assertion: Most patients with metastatic melanoma will be cured with current therapies.
items.reason: Despite improvements in therapy, most patients with metastatic melanoma will not be cured.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the reason correctly states that most patients are not cured.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · INITIAL APPROACH TO PATIENT WITH METASTATIC DISEASE | FOLLOW-UP AND SURVIVORSHIP

items.subtopic: NONMELANOMA SKIN CANCERS | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY
items.assertion: UVR is the most significant cause of NMSCs.
items.reason: There is a dose-response relationship between tanning bed use and the incidence of NMSC.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · NONMELANOMA SKIN CANCERS | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: NONMELANOMA SKIN CANCERS | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY
items.assertion: Immunosuppression is not associated with an increased risk of NMSCs.
items.reason: Solid organ transplant recipients have a significantly higher incidence of SCC and BCC.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true as immunosuppression increases the risk.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · NONMELANOMA SKIN CANCERS | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY

items.assertion: Keratoacanthoma should be treated similarly to other types of cutaneous SCC.
items.reason: Progression to metastatic SCC has been documented in some cases of keratoacanthoma.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why keratoacanthomas should be treated like other SCAs.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.assertion: Bowen's disease can arise on skin with minimal or no prior sun exposure.
items.reason: Bowen's disease is associated with human papillomavirus infection.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why Bowen's disease can occur in areas without significant sun exposure.
items.reference: Cancer of the Skin · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.assertion: Immunosuppression reduction can be beneficial in managing patients with multiple tumors.
items.reason: It helps control new lesions and manage existing ones by reducing the immunosuppressive load.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why immunosuppression reduction is beneficial.
